arguments.old_string:
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka log format. This interface abstracts over different * versions of the record format (magic versions 0, 1, 2, and higher) and provides access to batch metadata * and individual records. * *

Record Format Versions: *

    *
  • Magic V0 & V1: Older formats where a batch could contain a single record (uncompressed) * or multiple records (compressed). These versions lack producer ID, sequence numbers, and timestamps.
    • *
  • Magic V2+: Newer formats that support idempotent and transactional producers with sequence numbers, * producer ID/epoch tracking, and explicit timestamp types. These batches support both compressed and * uncompressed records.
    • *
* *

Role in Kafka Protocol: * Record batches are the basic unit of data in Kafka logs. They encapsulate a group of records with shared metadata * (compression type, timestamp type, producer ID, etc.) and provide efficient encoding of records using offset/timestamp * deltas relative to batch base values. This design minimizes storage overhead when records share similar offsets and timestamps. * *

Compaction Behavior: * During log compaction, base and last offset/sequence numbers are preserved for V2+ batches to maintain producer * state consistency, but individual records may be removed. This allows brokers to rebuild producer state from the log. * *

Thread Safety: * This interface does not mandate thread-safety. Implementations may be mutable (e.g., {@link DefaultRecordBatch}) * and should not be accessed concurrently from multiple threads without external synchronization. * * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the V2+ concrete implementation * @see MemoryRecords for in-memory collections of record batches */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

/** * Check whether this batch has a valid producer ID set. * *

For magic V0 and V1 batches, this always returns false as they do not support producer IDs. * For V2+, this returns true if the producer ID is not {@link #NO_PRODUCER_ID}. * * @return true if the batch has a valid (non-sentinel) producer ID, false otherwise */ boolean hasProducerId();

arguments.old_string:
/**
 * RecordBatch implementation for magic 2 and above. The schema is given below:
 *
 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
 * Note that when compression is enabled (see attributes below), the compressed record data is serialized
 * directly following the count of the number of records.
 *
 * The CRC covers the data from the attributes to the end of the batch (i.e. all the bytes that follow the CRC). It is
 * located after the magic byte, which means that clients must parse the magic byte before deciding how to interpret
 * the bytes between the batch length and the magic byte. The partition leader epoch field is not included in the CRC
 * computation to avoid the need to recompute the CRC when this field is assigned for every batch that is received by
 * the broker. The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial is used for the computation.
 *
 * On Compaction: Unlike the older message formats, magic v2 and above preserves the first and last offset/sequence
 * numbers from the original batch when the log is cleaned. This is required in order to be able to restore the
 * producer's state when the log is reloaded. If we did not retain the last sequence number, then following
 * a partition leader failure, once the new leader has rebuilt the producer state from the log, the next sequence
 * expected number would no longer be in sync with what was written by the client. This would cause an
 * unexpected OutOfOrderSequence error, which is typically fatal. The base sequence number must be preserved for
 * duplicate checking: the broker checks incoming Produce requests for duplicates by verifying that the first and
 * last sequence numbers of the incoming batch match the last from that producer.
 *
 * Note that if all of the records in a batch are removed during compaction, the broker may still retain an empty
 * batch header in order to preserve the producer sequence information as described above. These empty batches
 * are retained only until either a new sequence number is written by the corresponding producer or the producerId
 * is expired from lack of activity.
 *
 * There is no similar need to preserve the timestamp from the original batch after compaction. The BaseTimestamp
 * field therefore reflects the timestamp of the first record in the batch in most cases. If the batch is empty, the
 * BaseTimestamp will be set to -1 (NO_TIMESTAMP). If the delete horizon flag is set to 1, the BaseTimestamp
 * will be set to the time at which tombstone records and aborted transaction markers in the batch should be removed.
 *
 * Similarly, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the maximum timestamp of the current records if the timestamp type
 * is CREATE_TIME. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, on the other hand, the MaxTimestamp field reflects the timestamp set
 * by the broker and is preserved after compaction. Additionally, the MaxTimestamp of an empty batch always retains
 * the previous value prior to becoming empty.
 *
 * The current attributes are given below:
 *
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 *  | Unused (7-15) | Delete Horizon Flag (6) | Control (5) | Transactional (4) | Timestamp Type (3) | Compression Type (0-2) |
 *  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 */
public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Concrete implementation of {@link RecordBatch} for Kafka record format magic version 2 and above. * *

Binary Layout: * This class handles the serialization/deserialization of the following binary schema: * 

 * RecordBatch =>
 *  BaseOffset => Int64
 *  Length => Int32
 *  PartitionLeaderEpoch => Int32
 *  Magic => Int8
 *  CRC => Uint32
 *  Attributes => Int16
 *  LastOffsetDelta => Int32 // also serves as LastSequenceDelta
 *  BaseTimestamp => Int64
 *  MaxTimestamp => Int64
 *  ProducerId => Int64
 *  ProducerEpoch => Int16
 *  BaseSequence => Int32
 *  RecordsCount => Int32
 *  Records => [Record]
 *
* *

Compression: * When compression is enabled, the record data following the RecordsCount field is compressed as a single block. * Decompression is performed lazily when iterating over records via {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} * or eagerly in {@link #iterator()} to avoid resource leaks. * *

CRC Computation: * The CRC-32C (Castagnoli) polynomial covers all batch data from the Attributes field onwards (excluding * PartitionLeaderEpoch which is not included in CRC computation to avoid recomputation on broker assignment). * *

Compaction & Producer State Preservation: * During log compaction, this batch preserves base and last offset/sequence numbers from the original batch, * allowing brokers to rebuild accurate producer state. Empty batches may be retained to preserve producer * sequence information until a new sequence is written or the producer ID expires. * *

Timestamp Semantics: * BaseTimestamp represents the timestamp of the first record (or delete horizon for tombstone batches). * MaxTimestamp reflects the maximum timestamp of retained records (for CREATE_TIME) or the broker-assigned * log append time (for LOG_APPEND_TIME). * *

Batch Attributes: * * * * * * * * *
BitsField
0-2Compression Type (0=None, 1=GZIP, 2=Snappy, 3=LZ4, 4=Zstandard)
3Timestamp Type (0=CreateTime, 1=LogAppendTime)
4Transactional Flag
5Control Flag (control batches for transaction markers)
6Delete Horizon Flag
7-15Reserved
* *

Thread Safety: * This class is NOT thread-safe. The underlying ByteBuffer is accessed without synchronization. * Multiple threads must not read or write the same DefaultRecordBatch instance concurrently. * Thread-safe access requires external synchronization or per-thread instances. * *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Lazy Decompression: Use {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} to decompress * records on-demand, reducing memory footprint for large compressed batches.
    • *
  • CRC Computation: {@link #isValid()} recomputes the CRC every time; cache results * if validating the same batch multiple times.
    • *
  • Iterator Creation: {@link #iterator()} decompresses the entire batch immediately * for regular iterator support, while {@link #streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} defers decompression.
    • *
* * @see RecordBatch for the interface contract * @see MemoryRecords for managing multiple batches in memory */ public class DefaultRecordBatch extends AbstractRecordBatch implements MutableRecordBatch {

arguments.new_string:
/** * Write an empty batch header (no records) to a ByteBuffer. * *

This is used to preserve producer state during log compaction when all records in a batch * are deleted but the base/last sequence numbers must be retained for duplicate detection. * * @param buffer The target ByteBuffer positioned at the offset where the batch should start * @param magic The record format magic version (must be 2 or higher) * @param producerId The producer ID for idempotent/transactional batches * @param producerEpoch The producer epoch corresponding to the producer ID * @param baseSequence The base sequence number of the (now empty) batch * @param baseOffset The base offset of the batch * @param lastOffset The last offset of the batch (may equal baseOffset if only one record existed) * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch at the time of batch creation * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME) * @param timestamp The timestamp value (usually base timestamp or delete horizon) * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlRecord Whether this batch contains control records (transaction markers) * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic version is less than 2 or if timestamp is invalid * @see #writeHeader(ByteBuffer, long, int, int, byte, CompressionType, TimestampType, long, long, long, short, int, boolean, boolean, boolean, int, int) */ public static void writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, byte magic, long producerId, short producerEpoch, int baseSequence, long baseOffset, long lastOffset, int partitionLeaderEpoch, TimestampType timestampType, long timestamp, boolean isTransactional, boolean isControlRecord) {

arguments.old_string:
public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                   long baseOffset,
                                   int lastOffsetDelta,
                                   int sizeInBytes,
                                   byte magic,
                                   CompressionType compressionType,
                                   TimestampType timestampType,
                                   long baseTimestamp,
                                   long maxTimestamp,
                                   long producerId,
                                   short epoch,
                                   int sequence,
                                   boolean isTransactional,
                                   boolean isControlBatch,
                                   boolean isDeleteHorizonSet,
                                   int partitionLeaderEpoch,
                                   int numRecords) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Write a complete batch header with metadata to a ByteBuffer. * *

This method writes the fixed-size batch header portion (typically 61 bytes for magic V2+) * but does not write any record data. Record data should be written immediately after the header * at the buffer's current position after this method returns. * *

The method computes and writes the CRC over the attributes field through the end of the * record data (which may not yet be in the buffer when this method is called if records are * written sequentially). * * @param buffer The target ByteBuffer positioned at the offset where the batch header should start * @param baseOffset The offset of the first record in this batch * @param lastOffsetDelta The delta from base offset to the last record's offset (used to compute last offset) * @param sizeInBytes The total size of the batch including header and all records, with the {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} * constant subtracted (i.e., the size of the portion after the Length field) * @param magic The record format magic version (must be 2 or higher) * @param compressionType The compression type applied to records (NONE, GZIP, SNAPPY, LZ4, or ZSTD) * @param timestampType The timestamp type for records (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME) * @param baseTimestamp The timestamp base value (or NO_TIMESTAMP if batch will be empty; or delete horizon if delete horizon flag is set) * @param maxTimestamp The maximum timestamp of records (or for LOG_APPEND_TIME, the broker-assigned append time) * @param producerId The producer ID for idempotent/transactional batches (NO_PRODUCER_ID if non-idempotent) * @param epoch The producer epoch corresponding to the producer ID * @param sequence The base sequence number (NO_SEQUENCE if non-idempotent) * @param isTransactional True if this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlBatch True if this batch contains control records (transaction markers) * @param isDeleteHorizonSet True if the delete horizon flag should be set (baseTimestamp interpreted as horizon time) * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch at the time of batch creation * @param numRecords The count of records in this batch (0 for empty batches) * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic version is less than 2, if timestamp values are invalid, or if {@link TimestampType#NO_TIMESTAMP_TYPE} is passed * @see #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD for the fixed header size * @see #writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer, byte, long, short, int, long, long, int, TimestampType, long, boolean, boolean) for convenience method for empty batches */ public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, long baseOffset, int lastOffsetDelta, int sizeInBytes, byte magic, CompressionType compressionType, TimestampType timestampType, long baseTimestamp, long maxTimestamp, long producerId, short epoch, int sequence, boolean isTransactional, boolean isControlBatch, boolean isDeleteHorizonSet, int partitionLeaderEpoch, int numRecords) {

arguments.old_string: public static int sizeInBytes(long baseOffset, Iterable<Record> records) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Calculate the total serialized size of a batch with the given records. * *

This includes the batch header ({@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes) plus the serialized * size of each record with offset/timestamp deltas computed relative to the batch base offset * and first record's timestamp. * * @param baseOffset The base offset for offset delta calculation * @param records The records to include in the size calculation * @return The total size in bytes including header and all records, or 0 if records is empty */ public static int sizeInBytes(long baseOffset, Iterable records) {

arguments.new_string:
/** * Calculate the total serialized size of a batch with the given simple records. * *

Similar to {@link #sizeInBytes(long, Iterable)} but accepts SimpleRecord objects and * assumes a base offset of 0 with offset deltas incrementing from 0. * * @param records The simple records to include in the size calculation * @return The total size in bytes including header and all records, or 0 if records is empty */ public static int sizeInBytes(Iterable records) {

/** * Get an upper bound on the size of a batch with only a single record using a given key and value. * *

This is a conservative estimate for buffer pre-allocation purposes. The actual size may be * smaller due to variable-length encoding of record fields. Compression overhead is not considered, * so the actual compressed size may differ. * * @param key The record key (nullable) * @param value The record value (nullable) * @param headers The record headers array * @return An upper bound on the serialized batch size in bytes */ static int estimateBatchSizeUpperBound(ByteBuffer key, ByteBuffer value, Header[] headers) {

/** * In-memory collection of Kafka record batches backed by a ByteBuffer. * *

Purpose: * This class manages a contiguous buffer containing one or more {@link RecordBatch} objects, * providing iteration, filtering, slicing, and serialization capabilities. It is used throughout * Kafka for handling batches in producers, consumers, brokers, and administrative operations. * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Reading Existing Batches: Use {@link #readableRecords(ByteBuffer)} to wrap * an existing buffer for reading.
    • *
  • Creating New Batches: Use {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, Compression, TimestampType, long)} * or {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} for batch construction.
    • *
  • Log Filtering & Compaction: Use {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} * to filter records by retention policy during log compaction.
    • *
  • Network I/O: Use {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.GatheringByteChannel)} or * {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.TransferableChannel, int, int)} for efficient data transmission.
    • *
* *

Batch Structure: * The buffer contains a sequence of batches (one or more). Each batch encapsulates records with shared * metadata (compression type, timestamp type, producer ID, etc.). Batches are decoded lazily as they are * iterated; use {@link #batches()} to iterate over all batches or individual methods like * {@link #firstBatchSize()} to inspect specific batches. * *

Decompression & Iterator Semantics: *

    *
  • Streaming Iteration: {@link RecordBatch#streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)} * defers decompression until records are consumed, reducing memory footprint.
    • *
  • Regular Iteration: {@link RecordBatch#iterator()} decompresses the full batch * upfront, allowing iteration without resource management concerns.
    • *
  • Filtering: {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} combines * batch and record filtering, supporting log compaction and retention policies.
    • *
* *

Thread Safety: * This class is NOT thread-safe. The underlying ByteBuffer is accessed without synchronization. * Multiple threads must not read or write the same MemoryRecords instance concurrently. * For concurrent access, wrap each thread's iteration with external synchronization or use * thread-local instances. * *

Buffer Ownership & Lifecycle: * MemoryRecords does not own the backing ByteBuffer; the caller is responsible for: *

    *
  • Keeping the buffer valid during MemoryRecords' lifetime
    • *
  • Releasing/deallocating the buffer when no longer needed
    • *
  • Avoiding modifications to the buffer while iteration is in progress
    • *
* *

Performance Characteristics: *

    *
  • Lazy Batch Parsing: Batches are decoded on-demand during iteration.
    • *
  • Valid Bytes Caching: {@link #validBytes()} is computed once and cached; * invalidate by creating a new MemoryRecords instance if the buffer changes.
    • *
  • Decompression Buffers: Provide reusable {@link BufferSupplier} instances * to streaming iterators to reduce allocations for large compressed batches.
    • *
* * @see RecordBatch for the batch interface * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for constructing new MemoryRecords * @see DefaultRecordBatch for the V2+ batch implementation */ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {

/** * Get the total number of bytes occupied by complete, valid record batches. * *

This may be smaller than {@link #sizeInBytes()} if the buffer contains partial or incomplete * batches at the end (e.g., during network transmission or incomplete writes). * The result is cached after the first invocation; it does not reflect changes to the underlying * buffer. * * @return The number of valid bytes representing complete batches */ public int validBytes() {

     * @return first batch size including LOG_OVERHEAD if buffer contains header up to

     * @throws CorruptRecordException if record size or magic is invalid
     */
    public Integer firstBatchSize() {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Peek at the size of the first batch in the buffer without fully parsing it. * *

This method validates the batch header up to and including the magic byte, then reads * the batch size field. It is useful for: *

    *
  • Pre-allocating buffers for the first batch
    • *
  • Detecting incomplete batches during network reads
    • *
  • Quick validation without full parsing
    • *
* * @return The total size of the first batch (including {@link AbstractRecords#LOG_OVERHEAD} * which is added to the declared batch length), or null if the buffer does not contain * enough bytes for the header up to the magic byte * @throws CorruptRecordException if the batch size or magic byte is invalid */ public Integer firstBatchSize() {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Filter records into a destination buffer, applying batch-level and record-level retention policies. * *

This method is the core operation for log compaction and retention enforcement. It iterates * over batches and records, applying a filter to determine which batches/records to retain and * which to discard. Retained records are re-encoded into the destination buffer. * *

Filtering Semantics: *

    *
  • Batch Filtering: {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} is called * first to make a fast decision: DELETE (discard entire batch without inspecting records), * RETAIN_EMPTY (keep batch header even if all records are deleted), or DELETE_EMPTY (remove * batch if all records are deleted).
    • *
  • Record Filtering: For batches not explicitly deleted, {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)} * is called for each record to determine individual retention.
    • *
  • Batch Rewriting: If only some records are retained, the batch is re-encoded * with updated offsets and timestamps while preserving base/last offset/sequence for producer * state consistency.
    • *
* *

Delete Horizon: * If the batch contains tombstones or transaction markers and the original batch had no delete * horizon set, the filter assigns a delete horizon timestamp ({@code filter.currentTime + filter.deleteRetentionMs}) * to control when these records are eventually removed. * *

Buffer Overflow Handling: * If the filtered output exceeds the destination buffer's capacity, a larger buffer is allocated * internally and returned in the FilterResult. Callers should check {@link FilterResult#outputBuffer()} * and use the returned buffer if different from the input. * * @param filter The retention policy filter; provides batch/record decisions and timing for delete horizons * @param destinationBuffer The initial buffer to write filtered records to (may be enlarged internally) * @param decompressionBufferSupplier Supplier for decompression buffers. For efficiency with large * compressed batches, provide a supplier that reuses buffers (e.g., * {@link BufferSupplier} with pooling) rather than allocating fresh * 64 KB buffers for each batch. * @return A {@link FilterResult} containing the output buffer, metrics (messagesRead, messagesRetained, * bytesRead, bytesRetained, maxOffset, maxTimestamp), and the shallow offset of max timestamp * for use in stream position calculations * @see RecordFilter for implementing custom retention policies * @see FilterResult for interpreting the output metrics */ public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Write all records to the given channel (including partial records).
     * @param channel The channel to write to
     * @return The number of bytes written
     * @throws IOException For any IO errors writing to the channel
     */
    public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Write all records (including partial/incomplete batches) to the given channel. * *

This method writes the entire buffer contents to the channel, including any partial batches * at the end. It is a lower-level variant of {@link #writeTo(java.nio.channels.TransferableChannel, int, int)} * that does not skip incomplete data. * *

The method handles partial writes by repeatedly calling {@link java.nio.channels.GatheringByteChannel#write(ByteBuffer[])} * until all data is written. The buffer position is preserved via mark/reset. * * @param channel The target GatheringByteChannel to write to * @return The total number of bytes written to the channel * @throws IOException If an I/O error occurs while writing to the channel */ public int writeFullyTo(GatheringByteChannel channel) throws IOException {

/** * Create a builder for constructing a new MemoryRecords instance with default parameters. * *

Convenience method that uses the current (latest) magic version and CREATE_TIME timestamp type. * For more control over format and behavior, use {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long, long)}. * * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write records to * @param compression The compression type to apply to records (Compression.NONE, Compression.GZIP, etc.) * @param timestampType The timestamp type for records (usually CREATE_TIME) * @param baseOffset The offset of the first record in this batch * @return A new MemoryRecordsBuilder for appending records */ public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer, Compression compression, TimestampType timestampType, long baseOffset) { return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset); }

/** * Create a MemoryRecords instance with the given records in a single batch. * *

Convenience method using default parameters: magic version 2 (current), CREATE_TIME timestamps, * and base offset 0. For more control, use one of the other {@code withRecords} variants or * {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder}. * * @param compression The compression type for the batch * @param records The records to include (SimpleRecord instances are easier to construct than Record) * @return A new MemoryRecords instance containing the records in a single batch */ public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord... records) { return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, records); }

/** * Abstract filter for retaining or discarding records during log compaction and retention enforcement. * *

Filtering Two-Level Approach: *

    *
  1. Batch Level: {@link #checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} makes a fast decision * to DELETE (skip entire batch), RETAIN_EMPTY (keep header only), or proceed to record-level filtering. * This is efficient for large batches where all records share the same fate (e.g., expired segments).
    2. *
  2. Record Level: {@link #shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)} is called for each * record in batches that were not DELETE-d, allowing fine-grained retention control.
    3. *
* *

Common Implementations: *

    *
  • Log Compaction: Delete old versions of compacted keys, retain latest version.
    • *
  • Retention Enforcement: Delete records older than retention time/size.
    • *
  • Segment Cleanup: Delete all records in expired segments.
    • *
* *

Delete Horizon Semantics: * For records with tombstone values (no value) or transaction abort markers, the filter can set a * delete horizon timestamp ({@code currentTime + deleteRetentionMs}) to control when these * cleanup records are themselves removed. This delays deletion to allow consumers to see deletions. * *

Thread Safety: Not thread-safe; each thread should use its own filter instance. */ public abstract static class RecordFilter { /** * Current time in milliseconds, used for timestamp-based retention decisions * (e.g., "delete records older than this + configured retention"). */ public final long currentTime; /** * Retention time in milliseconds for delete/abort markers. Used to compute delete horizon * as {@code currentTime + deleteRetentionMs}. */ public final long deleteRetentionMs; /** * Create a filter with the given time context for retention decisions. * * @param currentTime Current time in milliseconds (typically System.currentTimeMillis()) * @param deleteRetentionMs Retention time for deletion records (tombstones and abort markers) */ public RecordFilter(final long currentTime, final long deleteRetentionMs) { this.currentTime = currentTime; this.deleteRetentionMs = deleteRetentionMs; }
